hypotheses: Individuals in long-term 'countries of immigration' (e.g., Germany, the United Kingdom) will exhibit higher distrust of politicians and political institutions than individuals in newer immigration countries.
Concern about immigration will have a stronger negative effect on political trust in countries with a long history of post-war immigration than in newer immigration countries.
The presence and strength of far-right parties will amplify the positive relationship between concern about immigration and political distrust.
claim.hypotheses_location: Section introducing specific hypotheses on immigration history and far-right influence (around pages 9–12 of the article).
claim.statement: Individuals who are most concerned about immigration’s impact on their national community are more likely to distrust political institutions in European states.
claim.statement_location: Proposition 1 paragraph in the theory section (approximately page 8).

data.wave_or_subset: All four ESS rounds; minorities and non-citizens excluded (≈4 % of observations) in main analyses
data.access_details: ESS data are publicly available via the European Social Survey Data Archive.
data.notes: Dummy variables created for long-term versus new immigration countries; several items reverse-coded and combined into indices.

method.description: The study applies multilevel (three-level) modelling to ESS survey data (2002–2009) to test whether concern about immigration predicts distrust of national political institutions after controlling for individual, country-round, and country-level factors. Instrumental-variables techniques are employed to address possible endogeneity between immigration concern and political distrust.
steps: Acquire ESS Rounds 1–4 microdata and remove respondents identified as minorities or non-citizens.
Construct indices for distrust in parliament, politicians, and the legal system; construct an index for concern about immigration; reverse-code items where necessary.
Generate individual-level control variables (unhappiness, interpersonal trust, economic dissatisfaction, ideology, demographic factors, far-right voting).
Create country-round-level variables (e.g., far-right vote share, social protection expenditure) and country-level variables (historical immigration dummy, GDP per capita, governance quality).
Estimate three-level hierarchical linear models predicting distrust outcomes with immigration concern and controls.
Test interaction terms for immigration concern with long-term immigration history and far-right party strength.
Conduct instrumental-variables analysis (using ESS Round 1 data) to check causal direction from immigration concern to political distrust.
Interpret coefficients, variance components, and significance levels; report robustness checks.
method.models: Three-level hierarchical linear models (multilevel regression); instrumental-variables multilevel models
method.outcome_variable: Political distrust (trust in parliament, trust in politicians, trust in the legal system—coded so higher values indicate greater distrust)
method.independent_variables: Concern about immigration (index of perceived economic, cultural, and quality-of-life impacts)
method.control_variables: Unhappiness, interpersonal distrust, dissatisfaction with economy, household income comfort, dissatisfaction with health and education services, left–right self-placement, far-right voting, winner–loser status, age, gender, education, GDP per capita, social protection expenditure, governance quality, historical immigration dummy

results.summary: Multilevel analyses show that concern about immigration is a robust, statistically significant predictor of political distrust across all three institutions examined (parliament, politicians, legal system). Coefficients remain significant after extensive controls. Interaction terms reveal that the effect of immigration concern on distrust is stronger in countries with long post-war immigration histories and, to a lesser extent, where far-right parties are strong. Instrumental-variables models support a causal interpretation running from immigration concern to political distrust.
